target.title: feat(telegram): opt-in interleaved progress lane
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Telegram renderer that projects reasoning text and structured runtime events into a single durable live message on the reasoning lane, matching CLI ergonomics without changing execution semantics.

**Gated on three conditions:** `toolProgressEnabled && interleavedProgress === true && hasReasoningLane` — inherits every existing group/DM/visibility gate.

## What it does

When `channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress` is enabled:
- Reasoning text streams into the interleaved message with a "Thinking" header
- Tool starts append a sanitized line (tool name only, never raw args by default)
- Item/plan/approval/command/patch events append title-only summary lines
- A rolling timer (20s cadence) shows elapsed time + a wall-clock stamp while running, so a stalled lane is visible at a glance; cleared on completion
- The model's reply is delivered once as the polished answer message — it is **not** mixed into the Thinking lane
- Long turns spill into continuation messages instead of freezing at Telegram's length cap
- Falls back to the existing `pushStreamToolProgress` lane when the flag is off

## Key design choices

- **Projection-only** — no changes to prompts, model routing, tool execution, or auth
- **No raw data** — tool lines show name only; `interleavedToolArgs` is a separate opt-in for sanitized args
- **No new event contract** — consumes existing `onReasoningStream`, `onToolStart`, `onItemEvent`, `onPlanUpdate`, `onApprovalEvent`, `onCommandOutput`, `onPatchSummary` callbacks unchanged
- **Reasoning + tools only; reply stays out** — the lane renders reasoning (`onReasoningStream`) and tool/event lines; the model's reply prose is **not** folded into it. Folding reply text in and stripping it back out at delivery proved unreliable — the model often drafts an answer, runs tools, then re-emits a revised one, leaving several answer-like copies of which only the last matches the polished final text. So the reply is delivered exactly once, as the answer. For the interactive backend the proxy restores real thinking on its own `onReasoningStream`, so reasoning is unaffected; a defensive final-answer strip remains for redacted-thinking backends that bridge assistant text into the reasoning lane. Tagged payloads (`<think>reason</think>answer`) keep only the in-tag reasoning, so answer prose after `</think>` never lands in the lane.
- **Single reasoning stream, delta-append** — appended as monotonic deltas (a cumulative snapshot contributes only its new suffix), so re-delivery never duplicates and the lane reads chronologically reasoning → tool → reasoning → …. The overlap fold is guarded: it folds a full-tail re-send (and full-increment duplicates) but never a short/mid-word coincidence, so a fresh fragment never loses its leading character.
- **Continuation on overflow** — as the durable message approaches Telegram's 4096-char cap the lane spills into a fresh continuation message (carrying recent context) instead of letting the draft stream stop editing and freeze the lane
- **Rate-limit-aware cadence** — the rolling "still running" timer ticks every 20s (not every few seconds) so N concurrently-streaming Thinking messages stay under Telegram's per-chat edit rate and don't draw 429s (a 429 drops the edit and stalls the lane); the line carries a wall-clock stamp so a genuine stall is obvious
- **No empty Thinking** — a turn that reaches final delivery with no reasoning/tool/status content (and no running timer) renders nothing, so the lane never emits a bare "Thinking" header
- **Plain-text transcript** — reasoning tag markers (`<think>` etc.) are unwrapped so the lane shows the model's prose, never raw tags

## Prerequisite

- **#80046** (CLI tool_use event bridge with sanitized args) — **merged 2026-05-29**; supplies the `onToolStart` lifecycle events the tool lines render. This branch should be rebased onto current `main` (which now contains it) before merge.

The renderer otherwise consumes only existing callbacks and is default-off, so it depends on no unmerged work. (On the standard `claude-cli` backend, CLI-*native* reasoning text additionally benefits from #85381's `thinking_delta`; without it the lane simply shows tool/event lines. The interactive backend restores thinking via its proxy, so reasoning appears regardless. Either way the default tool-progress lane is unaffected — this is not a hard dependency.)

## Changed files

- `extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts` — pure render helpers (gate, sanitize, header-strip, message render with length cap + no-content guard + wall-clock timer stamp, delta-append with cumulative-snapshot handling + guarded overlap fold, continuation-spill computation, defensive final-answer strip, status-line append, timer-interval constant)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — wire callbacks through the renderer with fallback; single reasoning-stream delta append (reply prose kept out of the lane); spill into a continuation message before the length cap; suppress an empty Thinking flush at final delivery; 20s rolling-timer cadence; reasoning-tag unwrapping; clear rolling timer in `finally`
- `extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts` — `stripReasoningTagsForInterleaved` (extract only in-`<think>` reasoning, dropping answer prose after `</think>`, preserving code regions)
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts` + `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` — opt-in flag schema
- Config surface docs + bundled channel metadata regeneration

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests: disabled-by-default, explicit opt-out, tool-progress-off, no-reasoning-lane, header dedup, rolling-timer lifecycle + wall-clock stamp, whitespace-collapse + length cap, delta-append no-duplication across a long cumulative stream, guarded overlap fold (no 1-char coincidence drop; full-tail re-send still folds), continuation-spill (repeated spills stay under the cap; giant-line force-advance; oversized-body tail-cap), no-content render guard, tagged-reasoning split (answer after `</think>` dropped; streaming/code cases), defensive final-answer strip
- [x] Live Telegram proof at current head (below)

## Verification

Local checks at the current head:

```
pnpm tsgo:extensions                                                 # typecheck — clean
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts \
                 extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts \
                 extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts   # lint — clean
npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.test.ts \
               extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.test.ts   # all green
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Opt-in Telegram interleaved progress renders reasoning plus tool/progress events in one durable Thinking message, without duplicating the final answer or freezing at Telegram message limits.

Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw Telegram gateway against Telegram, default-off feature explicitly enabled, two concurrent Telegram threads in one chat.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebuilt and ran the gateway from the PR head, enabled channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress, sent concurrent Telegram prompts that produced reasoning, tool progress, long Thinking output, and idle tool waits, then inspected Telegram messages and the gateway outbound Telegram send log.

Evidence after fix: Screenshot embedded below plus redacted outbound Telegram send-log metrics copied below: continuation msg=34783 -> msg=34786, final answer absent from every Thinking edit, zero bare Thinking sends, 20s timer edits at 04:20:15 -> 04:20:35 -> 04:20:55, and 99 outbound calls with 0 failures.

Observed result after fix: Thinking lane showed reasoning plus tool line, rolled into a continuation before the cap, sent the polished answer once as its own message, cleared empty Thinking output, and avoided Telegram 429s under the two-thread concurrent run.

What was not tested: No broad channel matrix or release package lane in this PR; focused local proof covers the Telegram renderer helpers, schema, lint, and extension typecheck.

## Live proof (current head)

Captured on the current head with the default-off feature enabled, over a **two-thread concurrent** session. The screenshot shows the visible lane; the rest is verified from the gateway's **outbound Telegram send log** — every `sendMessage` / `editMessageText` the bot issues — which shows message-level behavior a screenshot can't (message ids, edit cadence, final-state content, timing). Wire-level facts below are message-level metrics (ids, lengths, ok/timing) and contain no command/path/host content.

**Normal interleaving — reasoning + tool-use line in the Thinking lane, with the rolling timer:**

![interleaved lane](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/66da4d16b5a9467a82a65134976bc66aa956519c/proof-assets/interleaved-progress-telegram-2026-05-30-toolline.png)

**Wire-level verification (gateway send log, current head, one two-thread concurrent session):**

- **Long-message continuation** — `msg=34783` grew to the 4000-char cap and rolled into a fresh continuation `msg=34786` (carrying the next tool line) instead of freezing; clean seam, no garble.
- **No duplicate final answer** — on that long thinking-heavy turn the final answer was delivered once as its own message and is **absent from every Thinking edit** of `34783`/`34786`.
- **No empty Thinking** — **zero** bare-`Thinking`-header sends across the session.
- **20s rolling-timer cadence (rate-limit relief)** — while idling on a blocking tool, the "still running" line re-edited at **exactly 20s intervals** (04:20:15 → 04:20:35 → 04:20:55, body static at 3764 chars), keeping concurrent Thinking messages under Telegram's edit rate. The wall-clock stamp exposes any genuine stall.
- **No rate-limiting** — **99** outbound calls across the two concurrent threads, **0** failures (no `429`s, no oversized-preview stop) — the exact concurrency that previously drew 429s.

target.title: fix(agent): treat Anthropic long-context usage errors as context overflow for compact+retry
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opening as new PR after #52030 was auto-closed by barnaclebot for the 10-PR cap; reviewers and Greptile evaluated the fix positively at the time and the underlying bug still applies on current main.

Anthropic returns HTTP 429 with body `"Extra usage is required for long context requests."` when a request hits the long-context usage tier. Today the runtime classifies this through `isBillingErrorMessage` (which was extended in `failover-matches.ts` to match `/extra usage is required(?: for long context requests)?/i`), short-circuiting before any context-overflow handling. The right recovery is **compact + retry on the standard tier**, the session is too large for the standard usage tier, not a payment failure.

This PR adds `isAnthropicLongContextUsageError(message)` and inserts it into `isLikelyContextOverflowError` BEFORE the `isBillingErrorMessage` exclusion, so the long-context 429 routes to compact+retry rather than the billing fallback path.

## Files

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.ts`: new classifier + early-return inserted before the billing exclusion
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.ts`: re-export
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`: coverage for the new classifier and the overflow short-circuit
- `docs/gateway/troubleshooting.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`: document the compact+retry recovery path

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: An Anthropic HTTP 429 with body `Extra usage is required for long context requests.` is currently classified as a billing error by `isBillingErrorMessage` via the regex added in `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/failover-matches.ts`. That short-circuits `isLikelyContextOverflowError` and routes the request into the billing/model-fallback chain instead of the compact+retry path. The reorder in this PR detects the long-context body before the billing exclusion runs, so the runtime drives `compactAndRetry` on the standard tier instead of failing over.
- Real environment tested: live deployed openclaw gateway on rh-bot.lan, pid 71842, build SHA 23804e610c (upgrade-v2026.5.28 with this PR cherry-picked). macOS Darwin 25.5.0, Node v25.8.2, `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw`. The patched deployed bundle is the local production build of upgrade-v2026.5.28 with PR #84972 layered on top, staged under `/tmp/openclaw-pr-84972/dist/` on the same host as the running gateway; the deployed gateway dist at `/Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/` is the same source tree without this PR and is used as the pre-fix baseline.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```
  ssh alexm@rh-bot.lan
  ls -la /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/.buildstamp
  # -> {"builtAt":...,"head":"23804e610c998a0532e0c90441b130b38e5e8cc8"}
  ps -p 71842 -o pid,etime,command
  grep -l "isAnthropicLongContextUsageError" /tmp/openclaw-pr-84972/dist/*.js
  # -> /tmp/openclaw-pr-84972/dist/errors-Br4OcmFZ.js
  node /tmp/proof-84972-amittell-baseline.mjs   # pre-patch baseline against live deployed bundle
  node /tmp/proof-84972-amittell.mjs            # post-patch behavior against patched deployed bundle
  ```
- Evidence after fix: realistic Anthropic error payloads exercised against the patched deployed bundle on rh-bot.lan. Output of `node /tmp/proof-84972-amittell.mjs`:
  ```
  --- direct isAnthropicLongContextUsageError checks ---
  PASS longContext.isAnthropicLongContextUsageError: true
  PASS rateLimit.isAnthropicLongContextUsageError: false
  PASS promptTooLong.isAnthropicLongContextUsageError: false
  PASS unrelated.isAnthropicLongContextUsageError: false
  --- isLikelyContextOverflowError routing ---
  PASS longContext.isLikelyContextOverflowError: true
  PASS rateLimit.isLikelyContextOverflowError: false
  PASS promptTooLong.isLikelyContextOverflowError: true
  PASS unrelated.isLikelyContextOverflowError: false

  RESULT PASS (all checks)
  ```
  Output of `node /tmp/proof-84972-amittell-baseline.mjs` against the live deployed unpatched bundle on the same host:
  ```
  pre-patch isLikelyContextOverflowError(longContextMessage) = false
  BASELINE CONFIRMED: pre-patch deployed bundle misclassifies Anthropic long-context 429
  ```
- Observed result after fix: `isLikelyContextOverflowError("Extra usage is required for long context requests.")` returns `true` against the patched deployed bundle, where the same call against the live deployed unpatched bundle returns `false`. The canonical Anthropic context-overflow body `prompt is too long` continues to return `true`, the rate-limit body `Number of request tokens exceeds the rate limit` continues to return `false`, and unrelated server errors continue to return `false`, so the reorder only flips the long-context 429 case and leaves every neighboring classifier path untouched.
- What was not tested: end-to-end observation of an Anthropic 429 with the long-context body actually arriving at the deployed gateway and driving `compactAndRetry` through to a successful retry. Reproducing the upstream 429 requires a real Anthropic account above the standard usage tier and a payload large enough to trip the long-context check, which is not reliably reproducible from the lab hosts. The classifier surface that decides the routing has been exercised directly against the patched deployed bundle, and the equivalent in-tree unit suites under `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/` pass locally.

## Refs

- Superseded close: #52030 (barnaclebot 10-PR cap auto-close, no merit rejection)
- Original Greptile review on #52030 confirmed the misclassification path

target.title: fix(agents): forward turn-source for embedded plugin approvals (supersedes #84205)
target.body:
## Summary

Forwards turn-source routing fields through the embedded plugin `before_tool_call` approval chain so plugin approvals can be delivered back to the originating chat (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.). Without this, embedded-runner plugin approvals reach `requestPluginToolApproval` without a delivery route and resolve as `decision: null` (no-approval-route), blocking the tool while the model often hallucinates a fake `/approve` instruction and a synthesized success.

Supersedes #84205 (stale since 2026-05-19, blocked from updates because the PR branch is on `jzakirov/openclaw-official`). This PR carries the same intent as #84205, applied to the current `agent-tools.*` file layout (the upstream rename of `pi-tools.*` -> `agent-tools.*` happened after #84205 was opened), and extends it with two additional changes that turned up while validating the fix on a production deployment fork.

After ClawSweeper review the workshop fallback was tightened from `unavailableBehavior: "allow"` to the default fail-closed contract so mutating skill_workshop actions (`apply`, `reject`, `quarantine`) cannot bypass the operator-intended pending-approval default when no approval route is available.

## Details

Four commits, ordered by dependency:

1. `f5ef9d2b6f` "Allow skill workshop lifecycle without approval route" - extends the embedded-mode hook context with `turnSource*` fields and threads them through `requestPluginToolApproval`.
2. `713649e11e` "thread turn-source through embedded plugin approval contexts" - propagates `messageProvider` / `currentChannelId` / `agentAccountId` / `currentThreadTs` onto the embedded plugin `before_tool_call` hook context from every embedded entry point (codex app-server dynamic-tools and run-attempt, CLI runner, embedded-agent-runner, openclaw-tools, gateway tools-invoke-shared).
3. `5734dad75e` "thread turn-source through createOpenClawCodingTools factory" - threads the same fields through the factory that produces the HookContext consumed by every downstream plugin approval request. Without this third commit the call-site threading from commit 2 has no effect.
4. `ef5bb8d408` "block skill_workshop lifecycle approvals when no route is available" - removes the `unavailableBehavior: "allow"` carve-out per ClawSweeper P1 review. The default fail-closed contract now applies; the model sees an immediate `Plugin approval unavailable` block instead of a synthesized ALLOW_ONCE.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: plugin `before_tool_call` requireApproval requests originating from embedded-runner turns (CLI, codex, embedded-agent) had no `turnSource*` routing fields, so the gateway resolved them as `decision: null` (no-approval-route) and blocked the tool. The same fields were already wired for bash/exec approvals, so plugin approvals were the asymmetric gap. Observed in production on the deployment fork as Telegram-originated `skill_workshop apply` actions that never reached a deliverable approval prompt.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/turnsource-pr`, branch `fix/embedded-plugin-approval-turn-source-superset` rebased on `upstream/main` at `e59e65be67`. Build commit `ef5bb8d408`. Production gateways on `gateway-host` (`user@gateway-host`) are running the same commits cherry-picked into the deployment fork (`upgrade-v2026.6.2` at `3ef6591a4e`) and were verified at deploy time.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  cd /tmp/turnsource-pr
  pnpm build
  grep -c "turnSourceChannel" dist/*.js
  grep -F "Plugin approval unavailable" dist/agent-tools.before-tool-call-*.js
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts -t "skill_workshop"
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `dist/` bundle built from this PR head.

  ```
  $ git log --oneline -4
  ef5bb8d408 fix(skills): block skill_workshop lifecycle approvals when no route is available
  5734dad75e wip(agents): thread turn-source through createOpenClawCodingTools factory
  713649e11e wip(agents): thread turn-source through embedded plugin approval contexts
  f5ef9d2b6f Allow skill workshop lifecycle without approval route

  $ grep -c "turnSourceChannel\|turnSourceTo\|turnSourceAccountId\|turnSourceThreadId" dist/*.js | grep -v ":0" | head -10
  dist/agent-command-CIibMkGi.js:4
  dist/agent-CnqqLDfu.js:12
  dist/agent-delivery-CAkfTZyN.js:9
  dist/agent-tools.before-tool-call-C4q8C-bA.js:4
  dist/agent-tools-BvNXDsuT.js:4
  dist/approval-delivery-helpers-BCbYC_RR.js:1
  dist/approval-native-brfCkLin.js:4
  dist/approval-native-BI79BlFD.js:6
  dist/approval-native-route-coordinator-C4wbDCvi.js:5

  $ grep -F "Plugin approval unavailable" dist/agent-tools.before-tool-call-*.js
  dist/agent-tools.before-tool-call-C4q8C-bA.js:				reason: "Plugin approval unavailable (no approval route)",

  $ grep -A6 "function resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval" dist/agent-tools.before-tool-call-*.js
  function resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval(params) {
    if (params.toolName !== "skill_workshop") return;
    const action = readLifecycleAction(params.toolParams);
    if (!action) return;
    if (resolveSkillWorkshopConfig(params.config).approvalPolicy === "auto") return;

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts -t "skill_workshop"
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  2 passed | 22 skipped (24)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: (1) turnSource* fields propagate into the compiled embedded-runner bundle across 10+ files; (2) `resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval` in the compiled bundle no longer emits `unavailableBehavior: "allow"`, so a no-route approval for `skill_workshop apply/reject/quarantine` falls through to the default fail-closed branch (`blocked: true, deniedReason: "plugin-approval", reason: "Plugin approval unavailable (no approval route)"`); (3) the regression test on the post-fix branch passes both the original `reasoning` round-trip and the new `blocks skill_workshop lifecycle approvals when no route is available` assertion.

- **What was not tested**: a live Telegram round-trip of a `skill_workshop apply` against the running `gateway-host` was not driven to keep production traffic undisturbed. The compiled `dist/` bundle confirms the runtime code matches the source, and the fail-closed change is conservative (a stricter contract than the previous behavior), so the deployment risk is bounded to "skill_workshop runs without a live approval route now fail-fast instead of synthesizing ALLOW_ONCE".

target.title: Speed up precomputed command help startup
target.body:
## Summary
- add strict launcher fast paths for precomputed `doctor`, `gateway`, `models`, and `plugins` help
- keep container routing in control for those new help paths
- fix Windows `node --import` RSS hook paths in startup benchmark tooling

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --help`, `openclaw gateway --help`, `openclaw models --help`, and `openclaw plugins --help` now return precomputed help from the launcher instead of importing the full runtime before printing help.

**Real environment tested:** Windows 11 local OpenClaw checkout in `C:\Users\Yeqiu\Documents\agent-windows-lab\worktrees\openclaw-perf`, Node/pnpm toolchain from the repo, running the built CLI benchmark against the patched branch.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```powershell
node --import tsx scripts/bench-cli-startup.ts --case doctorHelp --case gatewayHelp --case modelsHelp --case pluginsHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --timeout-ms 10000 --output .artifacts/openclaw-startup-final-after-review.json
```

**After-fix evidence:** Copied live terminal output from the benchmark run:
```text
doctor --help: avg=50.5ms firstOutputAvg=42.1ms rssAvg=46.1MB code=0x3
gateway --help: avg=59.1ms firstOutputAvg=49.7ms rssAvg=46.2MB code=0x3

```

**Observed result after fix:** All four changed direct subcommand help invocations exited successfully and printed first output in about 42-52 ms with about 46 MB RSS. The previous direct `dist/entry.js` fallback path for the same help commands measured about 217-282 ms and about 76-77 MB RSS, so the patched launcher path avoids the full runtime import for these help-only invocations.

**What was not tested:** Full cross-platform CI matrix locally; focused launcher/runtime behavior, container fallthrough, startup benchmark tooling, and the copied live benchmark output above were tested locally.

- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write openclaw.mjs test/openclaw-launcher.e2e.test.ts src/entry.ts src/entry.test.ts`

## Summary

- Preserve explicit IDENTITY.md expression defaults (naming, vibe, preferred emoji) in the system prompt when IDENTITY.md is present
- Add a focused regression test for that prompt guidance text

## Changes

- `src/agents/system-prompt.ts`: detect IDENTITY.md in context files and inject guidance into the project context section
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`: regression test verifying IDENTITY guidance is present when identity file is loaded

## Scope

**2 files only** — no approval SDK, no action renderer changes, no unrelated fixes. This is the minimal scoped fix only.

Rebuilds from the closed #65777, scoped down to the real feature files only.

## Verification

Targeted test:
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — all tests pass

## Real behavior proof

Constructed the prompt locally on the PR head with a minimal `IDENTITY.md` context file and verified the new guidance line is injected into the Project Context section.

Reproducer (`./node_modules/.bin/tsx` against PR head):

```ts
import { buildAgentSystemPrompt } from "./src/agents/system-prompt.ts";

const prompt = buildAgentSystemPrompt({
  workspaceDir: "/tmp/openclaw",
  contextFiles: [{ path: "./IDENTITY.md", content: "Emoji: <redacted>\nName: <redacted>" }],
});

const target =
  "If IDENTITY.md is present, preserve its explicit expression defaults";
console.log("contains IDENTITY guidance:", prompt.includes(target));
```

Excerpt of the constructed prompt (line numbers preserved, identity content redacted):

```
   70 | # Project Context
   71 | The following project context files have been loaded:
   72 | If IDENTITY.md is present, preserve its explicit expression defaults such as naming, vibe, and preferred emoji in normal chat replies when they fit the context; do not suppress them by default.
   73 | ## ./IDENTITY.md
   74 | Emoji: <redacted>
   75 | Name: <redacted>
   76 | ## Silent Replies
   77 | When you have nothing to say, respond with ONLY: NO_REPLY
```

Assertion result:

```
contains IDENTITY guidance: true
```

The guidance line appears in the Project Context section exactly where the changed code injects it (immediately after the "context files have been loaded" header), confirming that loading an `IDENTITY.md` context file triggers the new behavior end-to-end.

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): provider usage-limits read accessor for plugins
target.body:
## What

Exposes a provider usage-limits read accessor on the existing `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-usage` subpath (no exports-map change):

- `getProviderUsageLimits(provider, opts?)` — awaitable, 60s cached.
- `getProviderUsageLimitsCached(provider, opts?)` — **non-blocking** stale-while-revalidate: returns the last value instantly and defers refresh to `setTimeout(0).unref()`, so a plugin reading limits at reply-delivery time never adds network latency to the reply path.

Both wrap the existing `loadProviderUsageSummary` + `resolveUsageProviderId` and map `UsageWindow` → `{ label, used_pct, pct_left, resets_in_s }`. Returns `undefined` for api-key / unmapped providers, leaving renderers free to self-source.

## Why

Plugins that render usage information (e.g. a per-response usage footer) currently have no supported way to read a provider's usage windows. This adds a narrow, cache-friendly read accessor for exactly that.

Motivating consumer reads `provider` from the `usageState` added in #89629 , then resolves the provider's usage windows for the footer's limits segment.

## Notes

- No new exports-map entry — adds to the existing `provider-usage` subpath.
- No code dependency on #89629 (neither imports the other); independently reviewable/mergeable. The pairing is only in the consuming plugin.
- Developed and running live on `v2026.6.1-beta.1`; cleanly cherry-picked onto current `main`. CI to confirm.

target.title: feat(queue): persist followup queues across gateway restarts
target.body:
## Problem

When the gateway process restarts (npm update, crash, systemd restart), any
messages that arrived while an agent turn was in progress are silently lost.
The followup queue lives only in process memory — there is no recovery path.

This is particularly noticeable on channels that serialize messages (e.g.
Telegram in queue mode): a message received during an active turn is queued
for delivery, then gone if the gateway restarts before that turn completes.

## Solution

Add persistence for the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global map in shared SQLite state
(`state/openclaw.sqlite`), matching AGENTS.md runtime queue policy.

### Core: `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.ts`

- `persistFollowupQueues()` — serializes all non-empty queue entries into
  `followup_queue_entries` on `openclaw.sqlite`. Respects `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`
  via the existing `resolveStateDir()` utility. Rows are deleted when all
  queues drain to empty.
- `restoreFollowupQueues()` — reads persisted rows at module init and
  repopulates `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` before any enqueue or drain fires.
- `PersistedFollowupRun` — a `Pick<FollowupRun, ...>` type that explicitly
  excludes runtime-only, non-serializable fields: `abortSignal`,
  `currentTurnContext`, `deliveryCorrelations`, `queuedLifecycle`. These are
  transient and must not survive a restart.
- Persisted routing selectors include `originatingReplyToId` and
  `inputProvenance` so restored runs keep reply-target context.

### SQLite layer: `src/infra/followup-queue-sqlite.ts`

- `replaceFollowupQueueEntries`, `loadFollowupQueueEntries`, and helpers used
  by persist/restore and doctor migration.

### Doctor migration: `src/infra/state-migrations.ts`

- Migrates legacy `live-chat-followup-queues.json` sidecars into shared SQLite
  and removes the JSON file when import succeeds.

### Mutation hooks

`persistFollowupQueues()` is called after every state-changing operation:

| Site | File |
|---|---|
| `enqueueFollowupRun` — after `items.push` | `enqueue.ts` |
| Drain `finally` block — both empty-queue delete and reschedule branches | `drain.ts` |
| `clearFollowupQueue` — after `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES.delete` | `state.ts` |
| `refreshQueuedFollowupSession` — after the run-rewrite loop | `state.ts` |

### Tests

`persist.test.ts`, `queue.followup-restart-roundtrip.test.ts`, and drain restart
tests cover:
- Shared SQLite rows written when queue has items
- Rows cleared when all queues are empty
- Full round-trip: enqueue → persist → simulated restart → restore → verify prompt + routing fields
- Runtime-only `abortSignal` field not present after restore
- No-op when no persisted rows exist
- Malformed entries (non-array items, null key) are skipped
- Telegram-routed followup redelivers exactly once after restart

## Notes

- The `draining: false` invariant is enforced on restore — any in-flight drain
  that was interrupted is restarted by the normal drain scheduler on next enqueue.
- `summarySources` (contains `FollowupRun` objects, not serializable) is reset to
  `[]` on restore. `summaryLines` (plain strings) is persisted and restored.
- Schema adds `followup_queue_entries` to `openclaw-state-schema.sql`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Queued followup messages received during an active agent turn survive a gateway restart and redeliver exactly once. Head `9ca2acbea` stores queues in shared SQLite (`followup_queue_entries`) and migrates legacy JSON sidecars via doctor.
- Real environment tested: Local dev checkout on Linux (`jewseppi/openclaw`, branch `feat/queue-followup-persistence`), Node 24, real temp state dir via `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, real `openclaw.sqlite` I/O (no in-memory mocks for persist/restore).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  cd openclaw
  pnpm exec vitest run \
    src/auto-reply/reply/queue.followup-restart-roundtrip.test.ts \
    src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.test.ts
  ```
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output (live module + real filesystem):
  ```
  RUN  v4.1.7 /home/joseph/dev/openclaw

   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  18 passed (18)
   Duration  2.53s

  Covered behaviors on head 9ca2acbea:
  - Telegram-routed followup queued mid-turn → persisted to shared SQLite → simulated gateway restart → restore → exactly-once redelivery → no replay after second restart
  - originatingReplyToId + inputProvenance round-trip through persist+restore
  - auth profile selectors preserved, secret-bearing config stripped
  - legacy live-chat-followup-queues.json migrated by doctor into followup_queue_entries
  ```
- Observed result after fix: After simulated restart, restored queue items retain prompt, Telegram routing, reply target, and input provenance; drain delivers the restored prompt once; post-drain persistence prevents replay on a second restart.
- What was not tested: Full live native Telegram desktop capture (Mantis run failed on shared QA infra). Contributor-side live Telegram E2E remains blocked by gateway/session serialization constraints documented in prior PR comments.

## Summary

- Fix `shortenHomeInString()` so it only replaces `display.home` when the match occurs at a real path boundary.
- Prevents incorrect shortening of home-like substrings such as `/srv/openclaw-home-other/dir`.
- Adds focused regression coverage for the boundary-aware helper in utils.test.ts.

## Why

- The previous implementation used unbounded substring replacement and could corrupt unrelated paths.
- This is a deterministic display formatting bug in the OpenClaw path helper.
- A real command invocation now proves the behavior outside Vitest.

## How

- Updated utils.ts:
  - `shortenHomeInString()` now scans for `display.home` and validates the preceding/following characters.
  - It only substitutes when the home token starts at the string or is bounded by a text delimiter, not when it appears inside another path segment.
- Added regression tests in utils.test.ts for:
  - a non-home-like path segment that must remain unchanged,
  - a true home-prefix path that still shortens correctly.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `shortenHomeInString()` was rewriting home-like substrings inside longer path segments, causing paths like `/srv/openclaw-home-other/dir` to be shortened incorrectly.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, Node 25.9.0, using `npx --yes pnpm` and `npx --yes tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  cd 'd:\Vs Code\AshyyHere\openclaw'
  npx --yes pnpm exec vitest run src/utils.test.ts --reporter=dot --no-color
  npx --yes tsx -e "import { shortenHomeInString } from './src/utils.ts'; console.log('/srv/openclaw-home-other/dir =>', shortenHomeInString('/srv/openclaw-home-other/dir'));"
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests  17 passed (17)
  /srv/openclaw-home-other/dir => /srv/openclaw-home-other/dir
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** the helper preserves `/srv/openclaw-home-other/dir` unchanged, while still shortening valid home-prefixed paths.
- **What was not tested:** full OpenClaw release gating, live gateway/runtime behavior, or sibling terminal-core display surfaces outside this helper validation.

## Testing

- `npx --yes pnpm exec vitest run utils.test.ts --reporter=dot --no-color`
- Result: `Test Files  1 passed (1)` and `Tests  17 passed (17)`

## Checklist

- [x] Tests added/updated
- [x] Real behavior proof included
- [x] No schema/config change
- [x] Fix limited to display/path-formatting logic

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds echo delivery infrastructure that mirrors session messages to additional channels
- `SessionEchoTarget` type on `SessionEntry` for persistent echo target configuration
- `message:sent` internal hook triggers echo delivery (fire-and-forget, no circular echo)
- CLI: `openclaw sessions echo add/remove/list` subcommands
- Gateway method: `sessions.echo` for programmatic pin/unpin

## Motivation

Sessions used across multiple transports (e.g. Telegram and the web UI) result in fragmented chat logs. Echo targets let every turn appear in all pinned channels regardless of originating transport.

## Files changed

| File | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/sessions/types.ts` | `SessionEchoTarget` type + `echoTargets` field |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo.ts` | Core echo resolution + delivery |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo-hook.ts` | Internal hook registration |
| `src/commands/sessions-echo.ts` | CLI subcommands |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` | `sessions.echo` gateway method |
| `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` | Method registration |
| `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts` | Hook auto-registration at boot |
| `src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.ts` | CLI command wiring |

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit test for `resolveEchoTargets` (deduplication, role filtering)
- [ ] Integration test: add echo target via gateway method, send message, verify echo arrives
- [ ] Verify circular echo prevention (echo delivery does not re-trigger hooks)
- [ ] CLI smoke test: add/list/remove echo targets

Supersedes #88386 (closed: branch had unrelated deployment commits).

---

## Proof (manual)

> Mantis isn't triggerable with my repo access, so this is supplied manually. Ran this branch live on a real Telegram bot (Windows, real session store). Covers the **integration**, **circular-echo**, and **CLI smoke** items in the test plan above. Telegram chat id redacted as `<chat>`.

**Build note:** the branch did not build/typecheck as-is — repaired in fd8ada2025 (now on this branch): duplicate `SessionsEchoParams`, stale `theme` import + `theme.bold`, `echoTargets` missing from the reserved slot-key list, and a wrong `.find`/`.filter` param annotation (`threadId` is `string|number`). `tsgo:core` clean after.

**1. CLI `add` / `list` / `remove`** (real store, net-zero):
```
$ openclaw sessions echo add  --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237 --label echo-proof
  Added echo target: telegram -> <chat> (echo-proof)
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --json
  { "echoTargets":[{ "channel":"telegram","to":"<chat>","threadId":"26237","label":"echo-proof","addedAt":… }] }
$ openclaw sessions echo remove --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237
  Removed echo target: telegram -> <chat>
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67
  No echo targets configured for this session.
```
Raw store reads confirmed `echoTargets: null → […] → null`.

**2. Live cross-channel mirror** (source thread 67 → pinned thread 26237):
```
[telegram] Inbound message …direct, 12 chars                            ← user message in thread 67
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36822 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed to pinned thread
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36824 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed
```
Echo fires off `message:received`, independent of the agent turn.

**3. No circular echo** — every echo delivery targets only the pinned thread with `silent=true`; none re-echoed. Structural guarantee: `fireEchoDeliveries()` omits `session`/`mirror` so the echo's own send never raises `message:sent`, so it cannot re-enter the hook (`echo.ts`).

---

## Update — surface-agnostic echo + live web→Telegram proof

Since the last review:
- **`sessions.echo.threadId` widened** to accept numeric thread IDs (Telegram forum threads arrive as numbers) + focused protocol coverage. (`20dba6e`)
- **Echo extended to the gateway chat path** so it fires for **control-ui / web / CLI** turns, not only channel-originated ones. A turn on a session with echo targets now mirrors **both** the inbound user message and the assistant reply to the tagged channel(s) — channel-agnostic, gated so channel-pipeline turns don't double-echo. (`73b6c75e` + the existing inline assistant-echo)

This realizes the design intent: **an echo target on a session mirrors that session's turns to the tagged thread no matter which surface resumes the session** (channel, web, or CLI), because every surface shares the same session key.

### Live proof — web app → Telegram (the original thread)
A message sent in a Telegram-originated session **from the web app** mirrored into the original Telegram thread:

![web to telegram echo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/echo88815-proof/echo-web-to-telegram-88815.png)

Gateway log (chat id redacted) — `echo-to-thread: 0 → 2` from one webchat turn:
```
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37041 threadId=67 silent=true   <- user message   (rendered "[via webchat]")
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37042 threadId=67 silent=true   <- assistant reply (rendered "[echo]")
cli turn ... outBytes=227 outHash=545140268128                                         <- real webchat turn (not a heartbeat)
```
Exactly two `silent=true` deliveries (user + assistant), no cascade — the no-loop guard held.

target.title: Add operator install policy and remove dangerous-code install scanners
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds operator-owned install policy enforcement for OpenClaw skills and plugins. Operators can configure a trusted `exec` policy command under `security.installPolicy`, and OpenClaw sends a structured request before supported install or update operations are allowed to proceed.

The policy applies to skill installs and plugin installs/updates from local paths, archives, Git, npm, ClawHub, gateway uploads, and managed or bundled paths where an install decision is made. ClawHub trust metadata is preserved as source context, but local operator policy is the final host-specific install gate.

This PR also removes the old install-time built-in dangerous-code scanner surface for both skills and plugins. `scan: false`, built-in dangerous-code install blocking, and force-unsafe bypass behavior are retired. The existing built-in plugin dependency denylist is preserved for compatibility and continues to block denied plugin dependencies before install. `--dangerously-force-unsafe-install` remains accepted on plugin install/update for compatibility, logs a deprecation warning, and is a no-op for dangerous-code scanning; it does not bypass operator install policy, plugin `before_install` hooks, or the plugin dependency denylist.

Diagnostic scanning remains available through `openclaw security audit --deep`, and plugin hook payloads keep an empty `builtinScan` compatibility field so existing hook handlers do not break on missing shape.

## Schema Reference

### Configuration: `security.installPolicy`

- `enabled`: Enables operator-owned install policy. When `true`, OpenClaw requires a valid exec policy configuration and fails closed if the policy command cannot be run.
- `targets`: Optional array of target types to enforce. Supported values are `skill` and `plugin`; when omitted, every supported target type is enforced.
- `exec.source`: Transport discriminator. Current value is `exec`.
- `exec.command`: Absolute path to the trusted policy executable. It is launched without a shell.
- `exec.args`: Static argument list passed to the policy executable.
- `exec.timeoutMs`: Total wall-clock timeout for one policy decision.
- `exec.noOutputTimeoutMs`: Timeout for a policy process that produces no stdout or stderr.
- `exec.maxOutputBytes`: Maximum captured stdout/stderr bytes before failing closed.
- `exec.env`: Literal environment variables to provide to the policy process.
- `exec.passEnv`: Environment variable names copied from the OpenClaw process into the policy process.
- `exec.trustedDirs`: Optional trusted directory roots for policy executable validation.
- `exec.allowInsecurePath`: Allows a command path that would otherwise fail path ownership or permission hardening.
- `exec.allowSymlinkCommand`: Allows the configured command path to be a symlink; the resolved target is still validated.

### Policy stdin

OpenClaw writes one JSON object to policy stdin:

```json
{
  "protocolVersion": 1,
  "openclawVersion": "0.0.0",
  "targetType": "skill",
  "targetName": "example-skill",
  "sourcePath": "/tmp/openclaw-policy/example-skill",
  "sourcePathKind": "directory",
  "source": {
    "kind": "clawhub",
    "authority": "official",
    "mutable": false,
    "network": true
  },
  "origin": {
    "kind": "clawhub",
    "requestedSpecifier": "openclaw/example-skill"
  },
  "request": {
    "kind": "skill-install",
    "mode": "install",
    "requestedSpecifier": "openclaw/example-skill"
  },
  "skill": {
    "installId": "example-skill",
    "installSpec": {
      "kind": "node",
      "package": "@openclaw/example-skill",
      "bins": ["example-skill"]
    }
  }
}
```

- `protocolVersion`: Policy protocol version. Current value is `1`.
- `openclawVersion`: OpenClaw runtime version that produced the request.
- `targetType`: Install target family. Current values are `skill` and `plugin`.
- `targetName`: Resolved human-readable target name or id.
- `sourcePath`: Staged file or directory available for policy inspection.
- `sourcePathKind`: Shape of `sourcePath`; current values are `file` and `directory`.
- `source.kind`: Source class. Current values are `archive`, `bundled`, `clawhub`, `file`, `git`, `local-path`, `managed`, `npm`, `upload`, and `workspace`.
- `source.authority`: Source authority classification. Current values are `openclaw`, `official`, `third-party`, `unknown`, and `user`.
- `source.mutable`: Whether the referenced source can reasonably change without the requested specifier changing.
- `source.network`: Whether the source depends on network retrieval or remote authority.
- `origin`: Source-specific provenance details, such as local path, upload metadata, ClawHub entry, Git URL/ref, npm package, or plugin package details.
- `request.kind`: Entry-point classification. Current values are `skill-install`, `plugin-dir`, `plugin-archive`, `plugin-file`, `plugin-npm`, and `plugin-git`.
- `request.mode`: Requested operation mode. Current values are `install` and `update`.
- `request.requestedSpecifier`: Original requested specifier when available, preserving what the user, CLI, API, gateway, registry, or caller asked to install.
- `skill`: Present for skill targets. Includes `installId` and optional `installSpec`.
- `skill.installSpec`: Parsed skill install declaration, when declared by `SKILL.md`; supported kinds include `brew`, `node`, `go`, `uv`, and `download`.
- `plugin`: Present for plugin targets. Includes fields such as `pluginId`, `contentType`, `packageName`, `manifestId`, `version`, and archive/file extensions when available.

### Policy stdout

The policy command must write one JSON object to stdout:

```json
{
  "protocolVersion": 1,
  "decision": "block",
  "reason": "Policy requires review before installing remote plugins.",
  "findings": [
    {
      "ruleId": "remote-plugin-review",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "plugin.json",
      "line": 1,
      "message": "Remote plugin installs require operator approval."
    }
  ]
}
```

- `protocolVersion`: Must be `1`.
- `decision`: Required policy decision. Current values are `allow` and `block`.
- `reason`: Human-readable explanation. Required for `block`; recorded in install errors and minimal policy logging.
- `findings`: Optional structured policy findings for logs and diagnostics.
- `findings[].ruleId`: Stable policy rule identifier.
- `findings[].severity`: Finding severity. Current values are `info`, `warn`, and `critical`.
- `findings[].file`: Optional path within the inspected payload.
- `findings[].line`: Optional one-based line number.
- `findings[].message`: Human-readable finding message.
- `findings[].evidence`: Optional concise evidence string.

Policy failures are fail-closed. Non-zero exit, timeout, no-output timeout, malformed JSON, unsupported protocol version, missing required fields, or excessive output blocks the install.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/dependency-denylist.test.ts src/plugins/install.test.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts`
- Focused Vitest coverage for install policy, plugin install/update, skill install, ClawHub install, archive/path/npm/git installs, plugin CLI flag behavior, and plugin dependency denylist compatibility.
- Focused lint and formatting for touched install-policy, plugin, skill, CLI, gateway protocol, and docs surfaces.
- `pnpm build`
- `npm pack`
- `$autoreview`
- Changed-lane proof through Crabbox local-container.
- Local host packaged live E2E: full local skill/plugin/update/upload/restart matrix, live ClawHub skill/plugin allow and block, diagnostic audit, and active plugin hook interaction.
- Linux container packaged live E2E: full local skill/plugin/update/upload/restart matrix, live ClawHub skill/plugin allow and block, diagnostic audit, and active plugin hook interaction.
- Native Windows 11 Parallels packaged live E2E: local skill/plugin allow and block, malformed and timeout fail-closed behavior, scanner-removal regressions, deprecated unsafe flag warning, and policy source-path availability.
- Docker Telegram live E2E using Convex-leased `telegram` credentials.
- Upgrade/residue live E2E on local host and Linux container: latest published npm release upgraded to the candidate package, stale scanner config rejected before repair, `doctor --fix` removed it, `config validate` passed afterward, and post-upgrade skill install, plugin install, plugin update block, fail-closed, and recovery behavior worked.
- Plugin dependency denylist live compatibility proof: package-installed candidate CLI blocked denied plugin dependencies with no operator policy, with an allow policy, with deprecated unsafe flag, and with vendored `node_modules`; a dangerous-code-only plugin without denied dependencies installed successfully.
- Plugin dependency denylist live upgrade proof: latest published npm package blocked the denied dependency first, then the candidate package installed over it and preserved the dependency block across no-policy, allow-policy, deprecated unsafe-flag, and vendored dependency cases.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Operators can enforce local install policy for skills and plugins, including ClawHub-sourced installs, while install-time built-in dangerous-code scanner blocking is removed in favor of explicit operator policy and diagnostic audit. Existing plugin dependency-denylist blocking is preserved.

Real environment tested: Isolated packaged installs on local host, Linux container, native Windows 11 through Parallels, broad changed-lane proof through Crabbox local-container, Docker Telegram live E2E with Convex-leased `telegram` credentials, upgrade-from-published-package proofs on local host and Linux container, and dedicated plugin dependency-denylist compatibility/upgrade proofs.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The verification commands and live package scenarios listed above were run against this branch and the generated package candidate.

Evidence after fix: Local, Linux, Windows, Telegram, upgrade/residue, plugin dependency-denylist compatibility, focused unit, build, lint, and changed-lane proofs completed successfully.

Observed result after fix: Supported skill and plugin install/update paths were policy-gated when install policy was enabled, policy source paths were available to the policy process, malformed/timed-out/unavailable policy responses failed closed, deprecated scanner residue was cleaned by doctor, dangerous-looking plugin/skill payloads installed when operator policy allowed the operation, and plugins with denied dependencies remained blocked before install.

What was not tested: Full release publishing and public release promotion flows.

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, retries the npm install, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

A deeper issue exists for the **interactive doctor** path (Clack-based prompts): Clack catches SIGINT internally, calls `guardCancel`, which calls `runtime.exit(0)`. This means the doctor process exits with code 0 (success), the installer marks `doctor_ok=1`, and proceeds with plugin updates and dashboard launch, even though the user pressed Ctrl+C.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers show a warning and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Fix `guardCancel` exit code**: change `runtime.exit(0)` to `runtime.exit(130)` in `src/commands/onboard-helpers.ts` so Clack-caught Ctrl+C exits with the correct SIGINT convention code, not success
4. **Add SIGINT flag defense**: before running the interactive doctor, temporarily swap the INT trap to set a `_got_sigint_during_doctor` flag. After doctor exits, check the flag. This catches the foreground-child case where the child catches SIGINT and exits 0, as an additional defense layer on top of the exit code fix
5. **Conditional dashboard open**: `should_open_dashboard` is set to true ONLY when `run_doctor` succeeds (returns 0). A failed or interrupted doctor never triggers the browser open. The flag is also reset before the final interactive doctor so a failed final doctor does not inherit an earlier migration doctor success.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during an interactive doctor prompt where Clack catches SIGINT and exits 0 no longer marks `doctor_ok=1` and no longer proceeds with plugin updates or dashboard launch. The `guardCancel` function in `onboard-helpers.ts` now exits 130 instead of 0, and the installer has a belt-and-suspenders SIGINT flag defense for the foreground-child case.

**Real environment tested**: macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw 2026.5.22 installed.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Shell harness sourcing install.sh with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1`, simulating the exact interactive-doctor code path. Test 1: a foreground child that ignores SIGINT (like Clack doctor) and exits 0, while sending SIGINT to the parent shell during child execution. Tests 2-4: exit-130, clean exit-0, and exit-1 paths. Test 5-6: static grep verification of `guardCancel` source.

**Evidence after fix**: Shell harness test output, 6/6 pass:

```console
$ bash /tmp/test-sigint-doctor-gate.sh

=== Test 1: Foreground child exits 0 after catching SIGINT ===
    (simulates Clack doctor catching Ctrl+C and exiting 0)
  result: ABORT
  PASS: SIGINT during doctor triggers abort (expected=ABORT)

=== Test 2: Doctor exits 130 (killed by SIGINT) ===
  result: ABORT
  PASS: exit-130 doctor triggers abort (expected=ABORT)

=== Test 3: Normal doctor completion (exit 0, no SIGINT) ===
  result: doctor_ok=1 should_open_dashboard=true
  PASS: normal doctor marks success (expected=doctor_ok=1 should_open_dashboard=true)

=== Test 4: Doctor failure (exit 1) ===
  result: doctor_ok=0 should_open_dashboard=false
  PASS: failed doctor stays false (expected=doctor_ok=0 should_open_dashboard=false)

=== Test 5: guardCancel exits 130 (not 0) on cancellation ===
  PASS: guardCancel uses exit(130) (expected=1)
  PASS: guardCancel no longer uses exit(0) (expected=0)

=== Summary: 6/6 passed, 0 failed ===
ALL TESTS PASSED
```

SIGINT during upgrade doctor phase (real upgrade on macOS with OpenClaw installed):

```console
$ timeout --signal=INT 10 bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1; echo "EXIT=$?"

  OpenClaw Installer

Detected: macos
Install plan: macos, npm

[1/3] Preparing environment
Node.js v24.15.0 found

[2/3] Installing OpenClaw
OpenClaw installed

[3/3] Finalizing setup
OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.5.22)!
Config already present; running doctor
Running doctor to migrate settings
Running doctor

! Installation interrupted
EXIT=124
```

**Observed result after fix**: Test 1 (the foreground-child SIGINT case, which is the exact scenario from the ClawSweeper finding) correctly triggers ABORT instead of proceeding with `doctor_ok=1`. The `guardCancel` exit code change ensures doctor exits 130 on Clack cancellation (not 0), and the installer's SIGINT flag provides defense-in-depth. SIGINT during any phase including the doctor phase triggers `abort_install_int`, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits 130 (124 when wrapped by timeout). No dashboard opens.

**What was not tested**: Full interactive install with a real TTY Ctrl+C during a live Clack doctor prompt (requires manual TTY interaction with real Clack prompts rendered). SIGTERM delivery (tested SIGINT only). Live CDN `curl | bash` path.

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
## Summary

Improve compaction identifier survival with transcript-wide extraction and quality-guard retry hints. Fixes two security/correctness issues found during review:

**Fix 1 — Sanitize string-encoded JSON arguments before extraction**: `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` now parses string-encoded tool arguments as JSON and passes the parsed object through `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before identifier scanning. Previously, string arguments (e.g. OpenAI's JSON-stringified format) were appended raw, allowing credential values like `{"token":"deadbeef12345678"}` to feed the hex identifier extractor and leak into `## Exact identifiers`.

**Fix 2 — Fail strict compaction when cap recovery cannot fit**: When strict mode detects that final capping removed required identifiers and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS`, compaction is now cancelled instead of returning a summary with known identifier loss. This prevents the session from continuing in a state where strict-required identifiers are silently dropped.

## Changed files

- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` — Parse string args as JSON, sanitize via `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before appending; non-JSON strings still pass through
- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts` — Cancel compaction (return `{ cancel: true }` with reason) when strict cap recovery overflows instead of silently skipping
- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts` — Three regression tests: string-encoded JSON credential sanitization, non-JSON passthrough, strict-mode cap overflow cancel

## Real behavior proof

### steps

Step 1. Create proof script exercising production `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers`, `extractOpaqueIdentifiers`, `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`, `computeLostIdentifiers`, and `capCompactionSummary`:

```typescript
import {
  extractMessageTextForIdentifiers,
  extractOpaqueIdentifiers,
  extractIdentifiersFromMessages,
  computeLostIdentifiers,
} from "./src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard-quality.js";
import { testing } from "./src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.js";
const { capCompactionSummary, MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS } = testing;
```

Step 2. Validate Fix 1 — string-encoded JSON args with credential `token` field:

```typescript
const msg = {
  role: "assistant",
  content: [{
    type: "functionCall", id: "call_1", name: "configure",
    arguments: '{"token":"deadbeef12345678","endpoint":"https://api.acme.io/v2"}',
  }],
};
const text = extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(msg);
assert(!text.includes("deadbeef12345678"));    // credential redacted
assert(text.includes("https://api.acme.io/v2")); // non-credential survives
```

Step 3. Validate Fix 2 — capping loses identifiers and recovery overflows:

```typescript
const transcriptIds = extractIdentifiersFromMessages([
  { role: "user", content: "deploy hash a1b2c3d4e5f6" },
]);
const oversized = "## Decisions\nKeep flow.\n## Exact identifiers\n"
  + "x".repeat(MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS) + "\na1b2c3d4e5f6";
const capped = capCompactionSummary(oversized);
const lost = computeLostIdentifiers(transcriptIds, capped);
assert(lost.length > 0);  // identifier at tail truncated by capping
const recovered = `${capped}\n\n## Exact identifiers (recovered)\n- ${lost[0]}`;
assert(recovered.length > MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS); // recovery overflows
```

Step 4. Run `npx tsx proof-75336.ts` from the patched branch.

### evidence

```console
$ cd /tmp/wt-75336 && npx tsx proof-75336.ts

[Proof 1] String-encoded JSON args sanitization
  PASS credential token value is redacted from string args
  PASS non-credential endpoint survives sanitization
  PASS hex identifier extractor does not capture redacted credential
  PASS non-JSON string args pass through for extraction

[Proof 2] Identifier loss detection after capping
  PASS transcript identifiers extracted from messages
  PASS hex identifier normalized to uppercase
  PASS capped summary respects size limit
  PASS identifier at tail is lost after capping
  PASS recovery block exceeds cap (triggers cancel in strict mode)

Results: 9 passed, 0 failed
```

### observedResult

Fix 1: `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` no longer leaks credential-field values from string-encoded JSON arguments. The `token` field value `deadbeef12345678` is redacted by `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before the hex identifier extractor can capture it. Non-credential fields and non-JSON strings pass through normally.

Fix 2: When strict mode detects identifier loss after capping and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS` (16,000 chars), compaction is cancelled with a diagnostic reason instead of returning a summary with known missing identifiers. The cancel reason is stored via `setCompactionSafeguardCancelReason` for downstream diagnostics.

What was not tested: Live compaction with a real model and conversation history (requires a running OpenClaw session with enough context to trigger compaction).

target.title: fix(gateway): skip pending reply drain on skip-deferral restart
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw gateway restart --safe --skip-deferral` skips the pre-restart active-work deferral check but still waits on the close-stage pending reply drain, because the `skipDeferral` flag is not threaded through the restart intent to the SIGUSR1 handler and server close logic.

The restart scheduler in `restart.ts` stores `skipDeferral` in module-local state (`pendingRestartSkipDeferral`) and uses it to bypass the deferral poll loop. But when it emits the SIGUSR1 restart via `emitPreparedGatewayRestart`, it passes no intent object, so `emittedRestartIntent` is `undefined`. The SIGUSR1 handler in `run-loop.ts` then gets a `restartIntent` without `skipDeferral`, and `resolveRestartCloseDrainTimeoutMs()` computes the full drain budget. The server close handler waits on pending replies from the restart-requesting turn itself.

The fix threads `skipDeferral` through the restart intent in **both** code paths:

1. **Timer path**: When `scheduledSkipDeferral` is true, passes `{ skipDeferral: true, reason }` as the intent to `emitPreparedGatewayRestart`
2. **Active-deferral bypass path**: When a `--safe --skip-deferral` request hits an already-active deferral poll, the bypass now also passes `{ skipDeferral: true, reason }` through the intent (was previously `undefined`)
3. Adds `skipDeferral?: boolean` to `GatewayRestartIntent` and `RestartIntentOptions`
4. In `resolveRestartCloseDrainTimeoutMs()`, returns `0` when `restartIntent?.skipDeferral` is true

Normal restarts (without `--skip-deferral`) are unaffected; the close drain timeout is computed from the drain budget as before.

Fixes #89528

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `--safe --skip-deferral` restart still waits on the restart-requesting turn's own pending tool/final replies because `skipDeferral` was not threaded through the restart intent to the server close logic. Additionally, a skip-deferral request arriving while a normal restart is already in the deferral poll loop would bypass the polls but still emit without the `skipDeferral` intent.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-89604`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Build from branch and verify fix is in compiled production code

Step 2. Verify restart scheduler threads `skipDeferral` through the intent via the timer path

Step 3. Verify restart scheduler threads `skipDeferral` through the intent via the active-deferral bypass path (a normal non-skipDeferral restart enters deferral polling, then a second skipDeferral request arrives and bypasses)

Step 4. Verify server close returns 0 drain timeout when `skipDeferral` is true

Step 5. Run tests to confirm behavior (including new active-deferral bypass test)

Evidence after fix:

Runtime exercise of the production `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` and `consumeGatewaySigusr1RestartIntent` from the built source, covering the timer path, the active-deferral bypass path, and normal restart:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-89604.ts
=== Skip-Deferral Intent Threading Proof ===

Exercise 1: Timer path -- skipDeferral=true, no preRestartCheck
  intent: {"skipDeferral":true,"reason":"gateway.restart.safe"}
  skipDeferral present: true
  reason preserved: true

Exercise 2: Active-deferral bypass -- skipDeferral=true while deferral is polling
[restart] request bypassed active deferral reason=gateway.restart.safe pendingReason=unspecified actor=<unknown>
  intent: {"skipDeferral":true,"reason":"gateway.restart.safe"}
  skipDeferral present: true
  reason preserved: true

Exercise 3: Normal restart -- skipDeferral=false (control case)
  intent: null
  skipDeferral present: false

=== Results ===
Timer path: skipDeferral=true produces intent with skipDeferral, drain timeout -> 0
Active-deferral bypass: skipDeferral=true now threads intent (was missing before fix)
Normal restart: no skipDeferral in intent, full drain budget applies
Both code paths verified: SIGUSR1 handler receives skipDeferral for skip-deferral restarts
```

All tests pass including the new active-deferral bypass test:

```console
$ npx vitest run src/infra/restart.deferral-timeout.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| ../../src/infra/restart.deferral-timeout.test.ts (10 tests) 36ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Observed result after fix: The production `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` with `skipDeferral: true` produces a restart intent of `{"skipDeferral":true,"reason":"gateway.restart.safe"}` through **both** the timer path and the active-deferral bypass path. The SIGUSR1 handler in `run-loop.ts` consumes this intent and the `resolveRestartCloseDrainTimeoutMs` function returns `0` when `skipDeferral` is present, bypassing the pending reply drain wait. Without `skipDeferral`, the intent is `null` and the full drain budget applies. Before this fix, the active-deferral bypass path called `emitPreparedGatewayRestart(undefined, reason)` with no intent, so the SIGUSR1 handler would compute the full close-stage reply drain budget even when `--skip-deferral` was requested.

What was not tested: Live `--safe --skip-deferral` restart with active pending replies from a channel-originated turn. The fix is verified at the intent-threading and timeout-resolution layers; end-to-end restart with live channel delivery requires configured channel credentials.

target.title: fix(acp): skip oneshot sessions during startup identity reconcile
target.body:
## Summary

Gateway startup identity reconciliation attempts to resolve pending ACP session identities. Oneshot sessions are terminal by nature, but their identity often remains `pending` after completion. The reconcile loop tries to ensure a runtime handle for each one, which fails and produces misleading warnings:

```text
[gateway] acp startup identity reconcile (renderer=v1): checked=7 resolved=0 failed=7
```

This makes it look like ACP/Codex/Claude agent routing is broken when fresh sessions are healthy.

## Fix

Skip sessions with `mode === "oneshot"` in `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` so the startup scan only processes persistent sessions that may genuinely need identity resolution.

## Changed files

- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.ts` -- Added `session.acp.mode === "oneshot"` guard after the session key check
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.test.ts` -- Regression test: oneshot session with pending identity is skipped (checked=0, no ensure/getStatus calls)

## Overlap with #85399

PR #85399 (bound startup sidecar fanout) rewrites the same startup reconcile loop. This PR's guard (`session.acp.mode === "oneshot" -> continue`) is an independent behavior concern from #85399's fanout bounding. Both changes apply cleanly on the same function. Landing order should not matter as long as the guard is preserved during any rebase.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ACP startup identity reconciliation processes oneshot sessions unnecessarily, producing misleading "checked=N resolved=0 failed=N" warnings and making ensure/reconcile calls for ephemeral sessions that will never need identity resolution.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from branch `fix/acp-reconcile-skip-oneshot` at commit fbdc594a0e.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. Installed dependencies in a clean worktree of the branch: `npm install`.
2. Created a proof script that imports the production `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` function directly from source and exercises it with three mock ACP sessions: one oneshot with pending identity, one persistent with pending identity, and one persistent with already-resolved identity.
3. Ran the proof script from the repo root:

```console
$ cd /tmp/wt-86711 && npx tsx proof-86711.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-86711.ts

Input sessions:
  [acp:oneshot-session-A] mode=oneshot, identity.state=pending
  [acp:persistent-session-B] mode=persistent, identity.state=pending
  [acp:persistent-session-C] mode=persistent, identity.state=resolved

  -> withSessionActor called for: acp:persistent-session-B

--- Results ---
  checked:  1
  resolved: 1
  failed:   0

--- Sessions that entered reconciliation (withSessionActor) ---
  acp:persistent-session-B

--- Verification ---
  Oneshot session SKIPPED (not reconciled):  PASS
  Persistent pending session reconciled:     PASS
  Persistent resolved session skipped:       PASS
  checked === 1 (only persistent-pending):   PASS
  resolved === 1:                            PASS
  failed === 0:                              PASS

== ALL CHECKS PASSED ==
The oneshot guard at line 43-44 correctly skips oneshot sessions
while persistent sessions with pending identities are still reconciled.
```

Observed result after fix: The production `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` function (imported directly from `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.ts`) skips the oneshot session entirely. Only `acp:persistent-session-B` (mode=persistent, identity.state=pending) triggers `withSessionActor`, `ensureRuntimeHandle`, and `reconcileRuntimeSessionIdentifiers`. The oneshot session `acp:oneshot-session-A` never enters reconciliation despite having a pending identity, confirming the guard at line 43-44 works. The persistent-resolved session `acp:persistent-session-C` is also correctly skipped by the existing `isSessionIdentityPending` check at line 48. Final counters: `checked=1 resolved=1 failed=0`, which would produce a clean startup log instead of the misleading `checked=7 resolved=0 failed=7`.

What was not tested: Live gateway startup with real persisted stale oneshot sessions from a multi-backend ACP deployment. That scenario requires an active ACP backend with accumulated historical Codex/Claude session data and would show the startup log line changing from `checked=N failed=N` to `checked=M` where M excludes oneshot rows.

target.title: fix(agents): recover tool calls from DeepSeek DSML text markup
target.body:
## Summary

Fix DeepSeek DSML tool-call recovery: ghost tool calls from truncated streams, duplicate tool calls when native `tool_calls` coexist, error wrapper content promoted to executable tool calls, and unbounded memory from oversized DSML blocks. Additionally enforces the 256 KB DSML capture cap in the close-token path, which previously allowed a single complete chunk to bypass the limit.

## Changed files

- `src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.ts` -- track DSML wrapper kind through capture; only `tool_calls`/`tool_call`/`function_calls` blocks produce executable tool calls (error wrappers are stripped but never promoted); add 256 KB capture cap to bound memory; enforce cap in close-token path before storing block; discard incomplete capture on final flush
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` -- skip DSML recovery when the response already contains native `tool_calls`
- `src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.test.ts` -- regression tests for error wrapper filtering, mixed executable/error blocks, oversized block discard, and oversized block with valid invoke markup (cap bypass regression)

## Verification

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.test.ts

 ✓ |unit-fast| ../../src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.test.ts (23 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

### steps

1. Built patched branch `fix/deepseek-dsml-tool-call-recovery` on macOS 15.5 arm64, Node v22.19.0, pnpm v11.2.2
2. Ran focused vitest suite importing production `createDeepSeekTextFilter` and `parseDsmlToolCalls`
3. Ran standalone proof script (`npx tsx proof-86637.ts`) that exercises three scenarios against the production filter module:
   - Scenario 1: Normal DSML block with invoke markup -> exactly 1 tool call recovered (`read_file` with `path: "/tmp/example.ts"`)
   - Scenario 2: Oversized (300 KB) DSML block containing structurally valid invoke markup -> 0 tool calls recovered (cap enforced at close-token path)
   - Scenario 3: Normal text without DSML tokens -> text passes through unchanged, 0 tool calls

### evidence

```console
$ npx tsx proof-86637.ts

--- Scenario 1: Normal DSML block -> tool call recovered ---
  PASS  visible text strips DSML: got "Hello  world"
  PASS  exactly 1 tool call recovered: got 1
  PASS  tool call name: got "read_file"
  PASS  tool call argument: got {"path":"/tmp/example.ts"}

--- Scenario 2: Oversized block with invoke -> discarded by cap ---
  PASS  no tool calls recovered from oversized block: got 0 (cap enforced at close-token path)

--- Scenario 3: Normal text -> passes through unchanged ---
  PASS  text passes through unchanged: got "Just regular text, no DSML tokens here."
  PASS  no tool calls recovered: got 0

=== Results: 7 passed, 0 failed ===
```

```console
$ npx vitest run src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.7 /private/tmp/wt-86637

 ✓ |unit-fast| ../../src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.test.ts (23 tests) 5ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  23 passed (23)
   Start at  14:39:42
   Duration  136ms (transform 31ms, setup 0ms, import 39ms, tests 5ms, environment 0ms)
```

### observedResult

1. **Normal DSML recovery works**: A well-formed DSML block with invoke markup is stripped from visible text and exactly 1 tool call is recovered with correct name and arguments. No duplicate native execution occurs because `openai-transport-stream.ts` skips recovery when native `tool_calls` are already present.
2. **Cap enforced at close-token path**: An oversized (300 KB) DSML block containing structurally valid invoke markup (`smuggled_tool` with a 300 KB payload parameter) produces 0 recovered tool calls. Before this fix, the close-token path stored the block without checking `MAX_DSML_CAPTURE_BYTES`, allowing the block to be parsed and promoted. The fix adds a size check (`dsmlCapture.length > MAX_DSML_CAPTURE_BYTES`) after appending the final content and before storing the block.
3. **Normal text passthrough**: Text without DSML tokens passes through the filter unchanged with no spurious tool call recovery.

Key regression tests (23 total):

- **"discards oversized DSML blocks containing valid invoke markup"** (new): sends a 300 KB `tool_calls` body with a structurally valid `<invoke name="smuggled_tool">` containing a padded parameter; asserts `recoveredToolCalls()` returns empty. This test would FAIL without the close-token cap fix because the invoke markup would be successfully parsed.
- **"discards oversized DSML blocks"**: sends a 300 KB `tool_calls` body (no invoke markup); asserts cap discard.
- **"does not recover tool calls from tool_use_error blocks"**: error content stripped but never promoted.
- **"recovers tool calls only from executable wrapper kinds"**: error + tool_calls blocks; only tool_calls invoke returned.

What was not tested: Live DeepSeek model streaming with actual DSML output (requires a DeepSeek API key). The proof exercises the actual production filter module through both vitest and a standalone script.
